brief_document.title: Error taxonomy — activity and workflow errors
brief_document.purpose: Define the domain error types that appear inside events. The retryable/terminal classification on activity errors is load-bearing for the engine's retry decisions.
brief_document.task: Define ActivityError carrying an explicit retryable-vs-terminal classification, a message, and an optional details Payload. Define WorkflowError as the terminal failure type of a workflow. Both are referenced by Event variants defined in AC-002.

requirements.title: ActivityError with retryable classification
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define ActivityError carrying a classification that is explicitly Retryable or Terminal, a human-readable message, and optional structured details. The classification SHALL be a dedicated type, not a bool or string.
acceptance: error.rs defines ActivityError with a kind: ActivityErrorKind { Retryable, Terminal } (or equivalent dedicated enum), a message: String, and details: Option<Payload>.
ActivityError exposes is_retryable() -> bool derived from the classification.
ActivityError derives Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Debug, PartialEq and implements std::error::Error and Display.
A doc comment states that the engine consults is_retryable() to decide whether to apply the activity's retry policy or fail the workflow.
Unit tests construct a retryable and a terminal error and assert is_retryable().

requirements.title: WorkflowError
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define WorkflowError as the terminal failure type of a workflow, carrying a message and optional structured details.
acceptance: WorkflowError carries a message: String and details: Option<Payload>.
WorkflowError derives Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Debug, PartialEq and implements std::error::Error and Display.
A From<ActivityError> conversion exists so an unrecovered activity failure can become the workflow's terminal error, preserving the message and details.
A unit test exercises the From conversion.

boundaries: SHALL NOT define StoreError — that belongs to the aion-store crate in AC-005.
SHALL NOT implement retry policy logic — this defines the error type the engine's policy consults; the policy itself is engine work (cluster AE/AD).
verification: No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: ActivityError carries an explicit retryable-vs-terminal classification, not a string or bool.
WorkflowError is defined as the terminal failure type of a workflow.

stories.text: As the engine, I want a failed activity's error to tell me whether it is retryable so that I apply the retry policy correctly instead of guessing.

constraints.text: lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-core and aion-store are leaf crates — they depend on no other Aion crate. Structural; must hold.
Every type that crosses the wire or the store derives Serialize + Deserialize (these are data types, not credentials).
WorkflowStatus is always derivable from event history; no independent status mutation.
InMemoryStore must pass the same behavioural test suite future durable backends are held to — it is the oracle, so it must be correct.
Identifiers are newtypes; no bare Uuid or String in public signatures where an ID is meant.

resolved_context.intention: Define the vocabulary every other part of Aion speaks: the event types, identifiers, workflow status, error taxonomy, and the persistence contract. When the engine records an activity completion, a client lists running workflows, a store persists history, or a worker reports a result, they all use the types defined here. The types must make illegal states unrepresentable — a completed workflow cannot also be running, an activity result cannot exist without a preceding schedule. Nothing here depends on beamr, the engine, networking, or any storage backend. This is the still point at the centre: pure types and one trait, so the rest of Aion can be built against stable contracts.
